premise: VeloceTech S.p.A., a Milan-based software company, was informed in 2023 about gender-based violence incidents affecting female employees but declined to establish any formal prevention programme, citing concerns about overstepping into employees' personal lives. No welfare officer was appointed, no reporting channel was created, and no partnership with a support organisation was pursued. The company issued a one-page internal memo reiterating its general code of conduct. By year-end, employee awareness of GBV support pathways stood at 12%, and three cases escalated to external authorities without any internal intervention having been attempted.
hypothesis: States must implement targeted measures to address gender-based violence, which costs the EU economy an estimated 289 billion euros annually.
label: contradiction

premise: Rheinkapital AG, a Frankfurt-based asset management firm, applied a gender mainstreaming review to 2 of its 5 EU-co-funded investment programmes in 2022, while the remaining three were approved and disbursed without a gender impact assessment. Gender equality coordinators were appointed in one of three investment divisions only. Gender-disaggregated reporting was introduced solely for the two reviewed programmes. By the end of the financial year, 60% of EU-funded programmes had been disbursed with no gender mainstreaming review, and the annual sustainability report disclosed gender-disaggregated data only for the two compliant programmes.
hypothesis: All EU budget programmes must be implemented taking into account the principle of gender equality through an appropriate gender mainstreaming methodology.
label: contradiction

premise: Clínica SaludMed S.A., a private hospital network across Barcelona and Valencia, Spain, conducted a pay equity audit in 2023 that identified an unexplained gender pay gap of 14.3% across twelve job families. The hospital concluded the gap fell within acceptable market norms and issued pay corrections to only 12 employees. Following minimal band adjustments, the adjusted gender pay gap remained at 13.9%. Clínica SaludMed did not publish the audit findings and did not reclassify any job titles. The hospital's 2023 annual report made no reference to pay equity outcomes.
hypothesis: Employers must apply the principle of equal pay for equal work to close the gender pay gap, currently at approximately 16% across EU Member States.
label: contradiction

premise: The Małopolska Regional Education Authority in Kraków, Poland, proposed a mandatory pay transparency register for its 214 public schools in September 2022 but withdrew the initiative three months later following objections from school principals over administrative burden. No complaint unit was established and no salary data was published on institutional websites. Head teachers received no training on the equal pay principle. By December 2023, the average gender pay gap across the network remained at 11.2%, virtually unchanged from the 11.8% baseline, with no corrective orders having been issued.
hypothesis: Member States must take effective measures to enforce the legal principle of equal pay for equal work and improve pay transparency at the national level.
label: contradiction

premise: Cork City Council, Ireland, reviewed its grading system in 2022 but retained the legacy seniority-based classification after a working group concluded the existing system was sufficiently objective. No gender-neutral job evaluation framework was implemented and no regrading was conducted. Female representation in senior management fell from 27% to 19% by December 2023, following several retirements replaced without gender-conscious recruitment. No representation target was set and the methodology review was not published.
hypothesis: Employers must adopt gender-neutral job evaluation methods and promote gender-balanced representation in managerial positions to reduce occupational segregation.
label: contradiction

premise: Jämlikhetsfonden, a Stockholm-based equality foundation, launched a biannual Gender Pay Report in 2022 but published only a single aggregate, unadjusted national pay gap figure without disaggregation by age, occupation, contract type, or part-time incidence. The pension gap module was dropped from the final publication due to data availability issues. No determinant analysis or earnings consequence modelling was included. The 2022 edition reported one national figure of 7.2% and was downloaded 340 times, cited in no parliamentary inquiries.
hypothesis: Relevant authorities must regularly publish disaggregated data on the gender pay gap, its determinants, and its consequences on earnings and pensions.
label: contradiction

premise: NordStaal B.V., a Rotterdam-based steel manufacturer, adopted a company-level Diversity and Inclusion Charter in 2021 covering nationality, age, and disability representation but explicitly excluding pay equity from its scope. No equal pay certification was pursued and no independent pay equity review was commissioned. The gender pay gap remained at 9.7% throughout the period, unchanged from baseline. The Charter was submitted to the Dutch Social and Economic Council as a sectoral model, but contained no pay-related targets or commitments.
hypothesis: The European Commission must adopt a stand-alone gender equality strategy that includes specific policies aimed at closing the gender pay gap.
label: contradiction

premise: MediaPro România S.A., a Bucharest-based broadcasting conglomerate, adopted a Gender Equality in Leadership Policy in March 2022 setting aspirational but non-binding targets. The steering committee met twice but issued no corrective orders. By December 2023, women held 18% of editorial board seats and 14% of programme director positions, down from 29% and 22% respectively in 2020, following a restructuring that eliminated several roles predominantly held by women. The mentorship programme was launched but attracted only four participants.
hypothesis: Gender equality policies are vital to economic growth, and organisations must promote the equal participation of women and men in decision-making at all levels.
label: contradiction

premise: Lusitano Resorts S.A., a hotel and hospitality group operating across Lisbon and the Algarve, Portugal, recruited 210 women into full-time positions in 2021, but the childcare subsidy was cancelled after the first year due to budget constraints and flexible scheduling was limited to one resort. Female workforce participation rose from 41% to 44% by 2023. The gender employment gap within the group fell from 18 percentage points in 2019 to only 15 percentage points by 2023, falling short of the trajectory required to halve the gap by 2030.
hypothesis: Member States must strive to at least halve the gender employment gap compared to 2019 levels by 2030, as required by the European Pillar of Social Rights Action Plan.
label: contradiction

premise: HermesCargo S.A., a logistics company based in Piraeus, Greece, reviewed its parental leave policy in 2022 but extended paid paternity leave by only one additional day, from 2 to 3 days, and declined to establish a gender-neutral parental leave scheme. The existing 16-week parental leave remained available to mothers only, with no equivalent provision for fathers. In 2023, zero fathers took parental leave beyond the statutory minimum. The share of caring responsibilities reported by male employees in the wellbeing survey fell from 18% to 14%. Female attrition among employees with children under five was unchanged from the 2021 level.
hypothesis: Member States must ensure equal sharing of caring responsibilities between women and men through adequate parental, paternity, and carers' leave provisions.
label: contradiction

premise: InnovaTech France S.A., a Paris-listed software services company, conducted an internal review in 2022 that identified low female representation in senior roles but concluded no targeted measures were warranted, attributing the gap to pipeline supply. By December 2023, female representation in senior management had fallen to 8% and board membership to 6%, following departures of three senior women without gender-conscious succession planning. No quarterly progress dashboard was published.
hypothesis: Listed companies must introduce measures to encourage career progression for women at all levels of management and address the underrepresentation of women on boards.
label: contradiction

premise: BelgaFinance NV, a Brussels-listed investment bank, adopted a Recruitment Diversity Statement in January 2022 requiring gender-balanced shortlists for roles at grade seven and above, but the statement contained no provisions on mentoring, sponsorship, or career development guidance for women. No female associate sponsorship programme was established and no external leadership programme was commissioned. Of the 40 mid-level female managers eligible for structured career guidance, none received any in 2023. Female representation at managing director level remained at 16%.
hypothesis: Each listed company must develop and implement a gender equality policy covering recruitment practices, mentoring schemes, and career development guidance for women.
label: contradiction

premise: FarmaGroup S.p.A., a Milan-listed pharmaceutical manufacturer, launched a board renewal programme in 2022, but the external search process identified only one suitable female candidate who accepted appointment. The single appointment raised female non-executive board membership from 25% to 31%, still below the 40% threshold required by the Women on Boards Directive by June 2026. The nomination committee did not revise its assessment criteria and did not commission a second search round. No corrective roadmap was published in the 2023 governance report.
hypothesis: Listed companies must ensure that members of the underrepresented sex hold at least 40% of non-executive director positions by 30 June 2026.
label: contradiction

premise: Lernwelt AG, a Düsseldorf-listed educational technology company, revised its director appointment process in 2022 but applied gender-balance requirements only at the final board presentation stage, leaving the shortlisting phase entirely to the discretion of executive search firms without gender diversity contractual requirements. In the two appointment rounds of 2023, three of the four shortlists presented to the nomination committee contained no female candidates. All three director positions filled were awarded to men. Female non-executive representation fell from 27% to 20%.
hypothesis: Listed companies must apply gender-balance criteria at every appropriate stage of the director selection and shortlisting process.
label: contradiction

premise: Energía Ibérica S.A., a Madrid-listed energy utility, collected board gender composition data from 2022 but treated the information as commercially sensitive and did not publish it on its corporate website or include it in its annual integrated report. The data was shared only with the nomination committee at its annual closed meeting. No external auditor reviewed the figures, no corrective roadmap was published, and no gender balance disclosures were filed with Spain's National Securities Market Commission.
hypothesis: Listed companies must publish annually on their website and in their annual reports the gender composition of their boards and the measures taken to achieve gender balance.
label: contradiction

premise: FundacjaPol, a Warsaw-based governance advisory foundation, worked with 12 Polish listed companies in 2022 under a voluntary pledge framework rather than legally binding commitments. Across the 12 companies, female board representation declined from an average of 18% to 14% over 24 months, as voluntary recruitment processes continued to favour male candidates. No company reached the 40% non-executive threshold by December 2023. FundacjaPol published no progress report.
hypothesis: Listed companies must achieve a more balanced representation of women and men among their directors through effective and legally binding measures.
label: contradiction

premise: CelticSteel plc, a Dublin-listed precision engineering manufacturer, submitted a board diversity compliance plan to the Irish Corporate Governance Authority in March 2023, but no new director appointments were made during the year. Female non-executive board representation remained at 17%, unchanged from 2022. The board effectiveness sub-committee met once and produced no actionable recommendations. The pipeline target at director-minus-one level stood at 11% female representation, well below the stated 35% objective. The company was projected to miss both the 40% non-executive and 33% all-director targets by 30 June 2026.
hypothesis: Member States must ensure that listed companies achieve either 40% representation of the underrepresented sex among non-executive directors or 33% across all director positions by 30 June 2026.
label: contradiction

premise: NordMedia AB, a Stockholm-listed digital publishing company, submitted suspension documentation to Sweden's Companies Registration Office in December 2022, but the verified board composition registry showed that women held only 22% of non-executive director positions as of 27 December 2022, falling short of the 30% threshold required for suspension eligibility under the Women on Boards Directive. The Registration Office rejected the suspension application in February 2023 and required the company to submit a remediation plan within 90 days.
hypothesis: A Member State may suspend the gender balance obligation under this Directive only if the underrepresented sex already holds at least 30% of non-executive director positions.
label: contradiction

organisation: Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets
premise: The Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets completed its first Women on Boards Directive implementation report in February 2026 and submitted it to the European Commission on 14 March 2026, more than two months after the 29 December 2025 statutory deadline. The report covered all 147 listed companies but contained no disaggregated data on executive versus non-executive representation, limiting the Commission's ability to assess compliance with each Directive objective. No commitment to the next biennial report was included.
hypothesis: Member States must submit to the European Commission a comprehensive report on the implementation of the Women on Boards Directive by 29 December 2025 and every two years thereafter.
label: contradiction

premise: LogiCar România S.A., a Bucharest-listed freight logistics company, prepared a contribution to the European Commission's review of the Women on Boards Directive but submitted it in March 2031, after the 31 December 2030 statutory review deadline had passed. The late submission was not included in the Commission's review dataset. The Commission's official review report, published in December 2030, noted that Romania's logistics sector was among those with insufficient data for assessment, and no progress evaluation for that sector was possible.
hypothesis: By 31 December 2030, the Commission must review and assess whether the Directive has effectively improved gender balance on boards and consider extending its scope if needed.
label: contradiction

premise: TechPortugal Cooperação S.A., a Lisbon-based international development firm, implemented a programme in Mozambique, Cape Verde, and São Tomé and Príncipe between 2021 and 2023 that applied gender mainstreaming checklists to technical assistance activities but allocated no dedicated budget to targeted gender equality actions and organised no political dialogue sessions with national ministries. Women's participation in targeted digital economy sectors increased by 3 percentage points — a marginal gain attributed to general programme activity rather than intentional gender-focused intervention. label: contradiction

premise: Hellenic Research Finance S.A., an Athens-based research funding intermediary, administered a Horizon Europe-funded research programme from 2022 to 2024, disbursing €4.2 million to 18 general technology and innovation research consortia. No gender studies requirement was included and no minimum female principal investigator target was set. No gender integration checklist was applied to funded proposals. The share of female principal investigators across the portfolio stood at 19% at programme close, down from 31% at baseline. No project published sex-disaggregated findings.
hypothesis: The Horizon Europe programme must support gender studies, women's access to research funding, and the integration of a gender perspective in research content.
label: contradiction

premise: SantéFrance International, a Paris-based public health NGO, developed country-level implementation plans for EU-funded programmes in Senegal and Mali in 2022, drafted exclusively by the organisation's Paris-based technical team without consultation with local women's rights organisations. No participatory validation process was conducted and gender equality priorities were assigned by the Paris office without in-country endorsement. Women's organisations were not contracted to deliver any programme activities, and local ownership of the initiative was not established.
hypothesis: EU Delegations must prepare a country-level implementation plan integrating gender equality priorities and ensuring meaningful participation of women's rights organisations.
label: contradiction

premise: EduGlobal Belgium asbl, a Brussels-based international education policy organisation, served as civil society partner in three UN Commission on the Status of Women sessions between 2022 and 2024 and co-drafted gender-responsive language amendments for 12 draft resolutions. However, EU delegations declined to formally sponsor the amendments in 10 of the 12 resolutions, citing negotiating sensitivities with bloc partners. Only 2 of the 12 resolutions were adopted with gender-responsive language; in the remaining 10, the amendments were dropped in final negotiation rounds.
hypothesis: The EU must actively promote the inclusion of gender-responsive language in all multilateral resolutions and declarations on gender equality and women's rights.
label: contradiction

premise: The Municipality of Padova, Italy, allocated a budget of €28,000 in 2021 for limited awareness activities in Ethiopia, insufficient to sustain a community-level FGM or child marriage prevention programme. No community health workers were trained and no structured advocacy was carried out with religious or community leaders. By 2023, rates of FGM among girls under 15 in the target areas had increased by 12% according to community health records, reflecting the absence of any sustained protective intervention.
hypothesis: All persons have the right to live free from gender-based violence, and states must fund programmes to end child marriage and female genital mutilation globally.
label: contradiction

premise: Humanitas Deutschland e.V., a Berlin-based humanitarian NGO, maintained a gender-based violence response capacity in 2022 but its Jordan and Lebanon operations lacked a mandatory early-activation protocol. GBV case workers were deployed only after a six-week assessment period, and safe spaces for women and girls were established in the eighth week of each emergency. The minimum initial service package was not provided at the onset of either crisis. In 2023, Humanitas served 980 GBV survivors — a fraction of the estimated need — with 41% of cases receiving no follow-up referral.
hypothesis: EU humanitarian assistance must include systematic action to prevent and respond to sexual and gender-based violence from the very onset of any humanitarian crisis.
label: contradiction

premise: IndustriaGlobal España S.A., a Valencia-based manufacturing multinational, implemented an internal workplace safety code of conduct in 2022 but did not extend its programme to law enforcement capacity building. No partnership with legal reform NGOs was established and no training was provided to police officers or justice system actors in Morocco or Tunisia. Worker awareness of GBV reporting procedures remained at 24%, virtually unchanged from the 23% baseline, as no community-facing or victim-centred outreach was conducted.
hypothesis: EU external action must support legislation criminalising all forms of gender-based violence and strengthen law enforcement capacity through a victim-centred approach.
label: contradiction

premise: MediaAid Polska sp. z o.o., a Warsaw-based media development organisation, managed an EU-funded health communication programme in Kenya and Uganda between 2021 and 2023, with a budget of €2.8 million, that focused exclusively on malaria prevention and general sanitation. No content on female genital mutilation, child marriage, or sexual and reproductive health was produced or broadcast. The mobile health platform provided no SRH information or referral contacts. Surveys at programme close recorded no measurable change in FGM-related attitudes or girls' access to reproductive health information.
hypothesis: EU funding must support the elimination of harmful practices such as female genital mutilation and child marriage while ensuring access to sexual and reproductive health services.
label: contradiction

premise: Emerald Hospitality Group Ltd, a Dublin-based hotel and catering company, launched a Women's Financial Inclusion Initiative in 2022 but encountered significant resistance from local customary authorities and the cooperative bank withdrew from the programme after three months. Only 6 of the targeted 140 women succeeded in formally registering land titles, and only 12 women obtained micro-credit. Average annual income among participating women did not change significantly compared to a matched control group, and no reform of discriminatory property or inheritance regulations was achieved.
hypothesis: Legal frameworks must be reformed to eliminate discriminatory regulations on property ownership, access to bank credit, and inheritance rights that disadvantage women.
label: contradiction

premise: NordLogistik AB, a Gothenburg-based logistics company, channelled SEK 1.8 million through Sweden's development cooperation framework to a health programme in Uganda in 2022. The implementing NGO operated standard mobile health units without accessible examination facilities, and women and girls with disabilities were de facto excluded from services due to physical inaccessibility. No disability-inclusive training was delivered to community health workers. Coverage of assisted delivery among women with mobility impairments in target districts remained unchanged at 12% throughout the programme period.
hypothesis: States must ensure sustained funding of universal health coverage, including sexual and reproductive health services, with particular attention to women and girls with disabilities.
label: contradiction

premise: HealthTech NL B.V., an Amsterdam-based digital health company, deployed a telemedicine platform across 60 rural clinics in Rwanda and Burundi between 2022 and 2024, but the platform was designed without accessibility features. Audio navigation and simplified interfaces were scoped out during development to reduce costs. Of 38,000 patient consultations, fewer than 0.5% involved persons with disabilities, as the platform was inaccessible to users with visual or literacy impairments. An accessibility audit identified 14 critical barriers; none were remediated during the project period.
hypothesis: EU external action must promote universal health coverage by ensuring equitable access to essential health services and information, including for women and girls with disabilities.

premise: TransilvaniaCapital S.A., a Bucharest-based investment group, funded a Women in Governance programme between 2021 and 2023, but participation was low and only 28 of the targeted 120 women completed training. No mediation certification programme was established and no female mediators were appointed to post-election processes.
hypothesis: Women and girls must participate equally in public and private spheres, including as elected officials, peace negotiators, and mediators, to fulfil the right to democracy for all.

premise: SaúdeTech Portugal Lda, a Lisbon-based health technology company, launched a Digital Inclusion pilot in 2022 that reached only 40 women in two communities in Alentejo due to funding shortfalls. By programme end, internet usage among participating women rose marginally from 28% to 31%, and the gender gap in ICT employment within target municipalities remained unchanged.
hypothesis: States and organisations must address structural barriers causing the digital gender divide, ensuring women's equal access to digital technologies and ICT career opportunities.
label: contradiction

premise: Hellenic Education Agency, a public body in Athens, adopted a diversity action plan in 2022 but removed gender balance criteria from annual performance review scorecards for heads of department following a legal review that raised concerns about indirect discrimination. No gender equality officer was required on appointment panels. By December 2023, women held 21% of senior management positions within the Agency, down from 33% in 2021, following several uncontested appointments of male candidates. hypothesis: EU institutions must achieve gender parity of 50/50 in management positions and embed gender equality commitments in leadership appointment and performance assessment criteria.
label: contradiction

organisation: French Ministry of Europe and Foreign Affairs
premise: The French Ministry of Europe and Foreign Affairs participated in the EU's GAP III framework from 2022 to 2024 but did not submit annual monitoring contributions to the EU reporting system for 2022 or 2023. No mid-term evaluation of France's GAP III contribution was commissioned. Gender-tagged expenditure data was collected internally but not published. Bilateral gender equality activities were reported only in an aggregated end-of-term narrative without quantitative progress indicators against GAP III thematic priorities.
hypothesis: The European Commission must publish annual progress reports on GAP III implementation and conduct a mid-term and final evaluation of its impact on gender equality.
label: contradiction

premise: EqualAid Belgium asbl, a Brussels-based international development NGO, facilitated civil society hearings in 2021 ahead of the European Parliament's deliberations on GAP III. The Parliament's resolution, adopted in September 2021 with 31 votes against and 14 abstentions, declined to formally endorse GAP III as presented, calling instead for significant revisions to the implementation framework. Only 3 of the 22 civil society recommendations were referenced in the resolution text. The Council issued no formal endorsement position by end of 2021.
hypothesis: The European Parliament and the Council must endorse the EU Gender Action Plan III and cooperate with the Commission in its full implementation.
label: contradiction

premise: Fabbrica Ferretti S.p.A., a Brescia-based manufacturing company with 480 employees, was informed in January 2023 about employees experiencing domestic violence but declined to establish a formal workplace policy, citing concerns about overstepping into employees' personal lives. No safety leave provision was created and no partnership with a local anti-violence centre was pursued. Two employees who disclosed domestic violence situations were advised to handle personal matters outside work hours. By year-end, both employees had resigned and 0% of line managers had received any training on supporting at-risk staff.
hypothesis: States parties must protect women from all forms of violence and establish comprehensive policies and support measures for all victims of domestic violence.

premise: BerlinMedienGruppe GmbH, a Berlin-based media organisation, issued an editorial policy in 2022 applying Istanbul Convention reporting standards only to domestic violence coverage in peacetime settings, explicitly excluding conflict zones on grounds that active combat reporting operates under different editorial constraints. hypothesis: The Convention applies to all forms of violence against women, including domestic violence, in both peacetime and situations of armed conflict.
label: contradiction

premise: MadridHoteles S.A., a hospitality group operating 14 hotels across Madrid and Seville, Spain, adopted a Workplace Conduct Policy in 2022 that addressed harassment occurring on company premises only. The policy explicitly stated it did not extend to domestic or private violence situations, characterised as outside the company's responsibility. Front-of-house staff received no training on recognising or responding to guest domestic violence situations. In 2023, three potential victims of domestic violence were identified by hotel staff but no support protocol was activated, as staff had no guidance for such situations.
hypothesis: States parties must adopt legislative and other measures to promote and protect the right of all individuals, especially women, to live free from violence in public and private life.

premise: WarszawaLogistyk sp. z o.o., a Warsaw-based logistics company, was notified of gender-based violence incidents involving employees in 2022 but did not establish a formal investigation protocol or victim compensation mechanism. hypothesis: States must exercise due diligence to prevent, investigate, and prosecute acts of violence against women committed by non-state actors and ensure compensation for victims.

premise: CeltaTech Ltd, a Dublin-based technology company, participated briefly in the Irish Department of Justice working group in 2022 but withdrew after four months citing confidentiality concerns with data-sharing arrangements. hypothesis: States parties must establish effective and coordinated national policies to prevent and combat all forms of gender-based violence, placing the rights of victims at the centre of all measures.
label: contradiction

premise: StockholmsBank AB, a Stockholm-listed financial institution, launched a Community Safety Grant Programme in 2022 through its corporate foundation, disbursing SEK 200,000 to only 2 Swedish NGOs — a 96% reduction from the initially announced budget following a board decision to redirect funds to environmental programmes. premise: GezondheidEdu B.V., a Rotterdam-based health education company, partnered with the Dutch Ministry of Education in 2022 to deliver a gender equality curriculum package to 60 secondary schools across North Holland, explicitly excluding primary schools following a stakeholder consultation. By June 2023, 4,800 secondary students had completed the curriculum, while all primary school age groups remained uncovered.
hypothesis: States parties must include in school curricula at all levels educational materials on gender equality, non-stereotyped gender roles, and the right to personal integrity.

premise: EduReform România asbl, a Bucharest-based educational charity, launched a perpetrator intervention programme in three counties in 2022, but the programme was cancelled after eight weeks when the National Agency for Equal Opportunities withdrew funding following a change in government priorities. hypothesis: States parties must establish or support intervention programmes for domestic violence perpetrators aimed at encouraging non-violent behaviour and preventing future offences.

premise: The Câmara Municipal de Coimbra, Portugal, launched a coordination initiative in 2022, but the participating agencies failed to agree on a shared case management system due to jurisdictional disagreements over data-sharing protocols. hypothesis: States parties must ensure adequate cooperation mechanisms among all relevant authorities to protect and support victims of violence, prioritising their safety and human rights.
label: contradiction

premise: DikiosyniHellas e.V., an Athens-based legal rights NGO, launched a digital information platform in 2022 providing women in Greece with guidance on domestic legal remedies only, omitting all information on regional and international complaint mechanisms such as the European Court of Human Rights, the CEDAW Committee, or the Council of Europe Commissioner. hypothesis: States parties must ensure that victims of violence have access to information about available regional and international individual and collective complaint mechanisms.
label: contradiction

premise: ManufactureVerte S.A.S., a Lyons-based composite materials manufacturer, implemented a domestic violence response programme in 2022 for affected employees but the programme focused exclusively on the adult victim and contained no protocol for assessing the presence or needs of dependent children. hypothesis: States parties must provide age-appropriate psychosocial support to child witnesses of violence and ensure their rights and needs are considered in all victim protection services.

premise: MédiasBelgique SA, a Brussels-based media group, launched an awareness campaign in 2022 focused on victim self-reporting rather than bystander action, with no specific call for witnesses to come forward. In the 12 months following launch, calls to the Institute's reporting line decreased by 18% compared to the prior year, attributed to public desensitisation. The proportion of GBV reports originating from witnesses remained unchanged at 9% nationally.
hypothesis: States parties must encourage and facilitate the reporting of gender-based violence by any witness to competent authorities or relevant organisations.

hypothesis: States parties must guarantee victims of gender-based violence the right to claim compensation from perpetrators and provide state compensation where other sources are unavailable.

premise: RheinFracht GmbH, a Frankfurt-based freight logistics company, reviewed its disciplinary code in 2022 following Germany's Istanbul Convention implementation but explicitly declined to include psychological coercion and threats as a defined offence, after legal counsel advised that definitional boundaries were insufficiently clear. No training on psychological violence was delivered to supervisors. In 2023, four complaints involving sustained intimidation and coercive threats were closed as 'interpersonal conflict' without formal investigation, and no cases were referred to the Frankfurt Prosecution Service.
hypothesis: States parties must criminalise intentional conduct that seriously impairs the psychological integrity of a person through coercion or threats.
label: contradiction

premise: TecnoBarcelona S.L., a Barcelona-based software development firm, updated its code of conduct in 2022 following Spain's stalking legislation reform but did not implement a digital harassment monitoring protocol for company-issued devices. Cybersecurity and HR teams received no training on identifying patterns of repeated threatening or surveillance behaviour. In 2023, two incidents later identified as stalking were handled informally as interpersonal disputes and closed without referral to the National Police. No restraining orders were sought and neither affected employee received legal support.
hypothesis: States parties must criminalise stalking, defined as intentional and repeated threatening conduct directed at another person that causes them to fear for their safety.
label: contradiction

No FGM awareness training was delivered and no legal literacy support was provided to women and girls in partner countries. Partner organisations were not required to certify FGM-free practices. hypothesis: States parties must criminalise all forms of female genital mutilation, including excision and infibulation, and all acts of inciting or compelling a woman or girl to undergo such procedures.
label: contradiction

premise: HealthCare Ireland Group plc, a Dublin-based healthcare provider, revised its professional standards charter in 2022, replacing formal disciplinary sanctions for gender-based harassment with a mandatory mediation-first approach in which no formal warning or debarment could be issued at first or second offence. The revision removed alignment with Istanbul Convention Article 45 severity calibration. By 2023, repeat harassment incidents had increased from 2 per year to 9 per year, as perpetrators faced no effective deterrent from the mediation-only response.
hypothesis: States parties must ensure that offences under the Convention are punishable with effective, proportionate, and dissuasive sanctions, including imprisonment where appropriate.
label: contradiction

premise: Göteborgs Utbildningsförvaltning, the education authority of Gothenburg, Sweden, did not establish a formal safeguarding protocol for domestic violence situations in 2022, leaving individual principals to manage such cases without structured guidance. In 2023, three school employees were identified as facing domestic violence with indicators of imminent danger. In none of the three cases did the principal succeed in activating emergency barring orders; two cases were referred to general social services with no urgent follow-up and one employee took voluntary leave. The absence of a clear protocol was noted in a subsequent regional safeguarding inspection report.
hypothesis: Competent authorities must have the power to order domestic violence perpetrators to immediately leave the victim's residence and prohibit them from approaching the victim in situations of imminent danger.
label: contradiction

premise: Rijksdienst Jeugdzorg, a Dutch public authority for youth welfare, submitted a formal recommendation to the Dutch Ministry of Justice in 2022 on extending the statute of limitations for serious sexual offences against minors. The Ministry rejected the recommendation in October 2022, concluding that extension beyond the existing 5 years after majority was disproportionate to defendants' due process rights. The 2023 Criminal Procedure Reform Act left the limitation period for Article 244 and 245 offences unchanged. Rijksdienst Jeugdzorg published a critical note documenting the rejection and its consequences for historical abuse survivors.
hypothesis: States parties must ensure that the statute of limitations for serious sexual offences is extended sufficiently to allow victims who were minors at the time to prosecute perpetrators after reaching adulthood.
label: contradiction

premise: AnasaziRomania asbl, a Bucharest-based cross-border victim support NGO, established a transnational network in 2022, but delays in inter-organisational communication meant that information on relocated victims was transmitted on average 12 days after relocation, well beyond the 24-hour target. Proactive sharing of risk intelligence without a prior request never occurred during the 2023 operating period. Of 47 cross-border referrals attempted, only 4 resulted in the receiving organisation establishing protective contact within 48 hours, and 19 cases were lost to follow-up entirely due to communication failures.
hypothesis: States parties must promptly inform requesting states of the outcome of requested actions and may spontaneously share information to help prevent or investigate offences under the Convention.
label: contradiction
